canonical_summary:
Space compliance in China is governed by a multi-agency stack rather than a single space law. Civil space policy and international cooperation run through the China National Space Administration (CNSA), which operates as a bureau under the State Administration of Science, Technology and Industry for National Defense (SASTIND), itself a vice-ministerial body inside the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT). Human spaceflight sits in a separate lineage under the China Manned Space Agency (CMSA), reporting through the Central Military Commission's Equipment Development Department. SASTIND issues civil launch permits under Order No. 12 of 2002, while MIIT controls every commercial satellite operator's path to revenue through spectrum, station, and services licensing administered by its Radio Management Bureau.

The operative 2024 to 2026 regulatory output includes MIIT's Interim Measures for Domestic Coordination and Management of Satellite Networks (in force 1 May 2025), the August 2024 Guiding Opinions on Optimizing Business Access for the Satellite Communications Industry, batched space radio station licensing for low Earth orbit constellations, and SASTIND's tightening notice layering debris mitigation, passivation, and post-launch reporting on Order No. 12. The 2024 Negative List (NDRC and MOFCOM Order No. 23, effective 1 November 2024) liberalizes manufacturing but leaves the binding constraints on foreign investors off-list: weapons-equipment licensing for launch vehicles, Chinese-control rules for basic telecommunications, and a flat prohibition on foreign-invested surveying and mapping.
meta_title: Space compliance in China: regulators and licensing
meta_description: Authoritative reference on China space compliance: CNSA, CMSA, SASTIND, MIIT licensing, the 2024 Negative List, and what international operators must clear.

sections.heading: The Chinese space regulatory stack: CNSA, CMSA, MIIT, SASTIND
paragraphs: China's space activity is governed by a tri-partite structure that often confuses outside observers because the agencies share personnel, share facilities, and in some cases share offices, while answering to very different masters. The China National Space Administration (CNSA) and the China Manned Space Agency (CMSA, also rendered CMS or CMSEO) sit at the visible front of the program, but neither is a stand-alone ministry.

CNSA is administratively a bureau under the State Administration of Science, Technology and Industry for National Defense (SASTIND), which itself is a vice-ministerial body inside the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT). It was carved out of the Ministry of Aerospace Industry in 1993 so that China would have a civilian counterpart capable of signing inter-agency agreements with NASA, ESA, JAXA, and Roscosmos. CNSA shares leadership, staff, and its compound with SASTIND. Administrator Shan Zhongde is concurrently a Vice Minister of MIIT and a Deputy Director of SASTIND, the standard pattern since 2008.

CMSA sits in a different lineage entirely. Established in June 1993 to administer Project 921, it reports through the Central Military Commission's Equipment Development Department, not through SASTIND or MIIT. The astronaut corps is military, Jiuquan is a PLA range, and the Tiangong space station's operational chain of command runs through Beijing Aerospace Control Center, also a PLA facility. CMSA coordinates with civilian players through a State Council and CMC joint leading group.

SASTIND, established in its current form in the 2008 super-ministry reform that folded the old COSTIND into MIIT, plays the connective role. It writes the regulatory framework that binds CNSA-led civil missions, licenses commercial launches by private actors such as LandSpace and Galactic Energy, allocates defense research budgets across CASC and CASIC, and oversees the dual-use industrial base.

subsections.heading: CNSA: civilian face, SASTIND backbone
paragraphs: CNSA's own organization page describes the agency as responsible for the management of space activities for civilian use and international space cooperation. Its five statutory functions are studying and formulating policies and regulations of the space industry, studying and formulating development programs and plans of the space industry, organizing and implementing major space projects and programs, demonstrating, approving, implementing and supervising civilian space scientific research projects, and managing international space exchanges and cooperation.

CNSA does not have an independent budget line or independent legal personality. It writes civilian policy, demonstrates and approves civil science missions (the Chang'e lunar series, the Tianwen planetary series, the China Seismo-Electromagnetic Satellite), and handles inter-governmental cooperation. Engineering, launches, and operations are done by CASC subsidiaries under contracts that SASTIND ultimately supervises.

subsections.heading: CMSA: outside SASTIND, inside the military
paragraphs: CMSA's self-listed remit is unified management of the China Manned Space Program including development strategy, planning, overall technology, research and production, infrastructure construction, flight missions organization and implementation, utilization and promotion, international cooperation, and news release. It is internally organized into an Integrated Planning and Management Division, a Scientific Program and Quality Control Division, a Utilization and Development Division, an Infrastructure Construction Division, and a System Division.

English-language briefings often refer to CMS and CMSEO interchangeably. CMSEO, the China Manned Space Engineering Office, is the executive arm sitting under CMSA, and both names appear on the same official website.

sections.heading: Launch licensing under SASTIND
paragraphs: China's civil and de facto commercial launch licensing regime is anchored in the Interim Measures on the Administration of Permits for Civil Space Launch Projects. The instrument is Order No. 12 of the Commission of Science, Technology and Industry for National Defense (COSTIND), signed 21 November 2002 and effective 21 December 2002. COSTIND was reorganized in 2008 into SASTIND, which inherited administration of the Measures. The legal pedigree sits inside Item 29 of the annex to State Council Order No. 412.

Article 2 applies to any natural person, legal person, or other organization undertaking civil space launch projects from within Chinese territory, or carrying out such projects abroad using assets owned by Chinese entities. Each individual launch project requires its own permit; permits are non-transferable, project-specific, and expire automatically on project completion. Article 6 requires the launch operator to submit application materials nine months before the planned launch month. For cross-departmental coordination, the project plan must additionally be reported six months in advance. SASTIND conducts substantive examination within 30 days of receiving a complete application and notifies the applicant of the decision within 10 working days thereafter.

subsections.heading: Substantive approval criteria
bullets: Compliance with Chinese law and state secrecy obligations.
No threat to national security, foreign policy, or international treaty obligations under the Outer Space Treaty, Registration Convention, and Liability Convention.
No likelihood of irreparable harm to public interests.
Possession of all upstream sectoral permits, including radio frequency authorization from the radio regulatory authority.
Technical and financial capacity commensurate with the mission.
Mandatory third-party liability insurance for the launched space object, plus other prescribed coverage.
paragraphs: Applications are refused where the applicant has had a major secrecy breach within the prior year, has had a permit revoked within the prior two years, or lacks radio licensing or insurance.

subsections.heading: The SASTIND tightening notice
paragraphs: SASTIND has periodically issued binding notices on strengthening the management of civil space launch project licenses, most recently in early 2023, layering eleven further requirements onto Order No. 12. The application package must now include the third-party liability insurance certificate, the radio frequency license, a debris mitigation plan, and payload identity data covering name, category, dimensions, mass, owner, and operator.

After launch, license holders must file a project completion report within 30 days, deorbit satellites at end-of-life, passivate rocket upper stages, give 30 days' notice for any life-extension request, and report orbital changes, fragmentations, or reentries. Uncovered third-party loss remains the holder's residual liability, and fraud or violation can trigger license revocation and criminal referral.

subsections.heading: Registration of space objects
paragraphs: The licensing track sits beside the Measures for the Registration of Objects Launched into Outer Space, jointly issued by COSTIND and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs as Order No. 6 of 8 February 2001. Registration with SASTIND is the predicate for China's notification to the UN Secretary-General under the 1975 Registration Convention. The licensee is responsible for filing registration data after a successful launch.

sections.heading: Satellite operations under MIIT
paragraphs: MIIT is the central civilian regulator for commercial satellite communications, radio spectrum, and orbital coordination. While launch licensing and on-orbit activity sit primarily with SASTIND and CNSA, MIIT controls the gate that every commercial satellite operator must pass through: assignment of radio frequencies, issuance of space and earth radio station licenses, domestic coordination of satellite networks declared to the ITU, and the telecom-services market access permits that allow constellations to sell connectivity inside China.

The licensing stack is administered through MIIT's Radio Management Bureau, whose mandate covers radio frequency planning, spectrum allocation and assignment, satellite orbital-position coordination, and ITU-facing radio matters.

subsections.heading: The four MIIT gates
bullets: Radio Frequency Use Permit for the satellite's downlink and uplink bands.
Space Radio Station License for each on-orbit transmitter.
Satellite Earth Station Setup and Use License for gateways and user terminals, processed through the MIIT government-services portal.
Telecom services market entry permit through the Telecom Business Market Integrated Management Information System where the operator sells connectivity.

subsections.heading: 2024 to 2025 batched licensing for constellations
paragraphs: Through 2024 MIIT visibly accelerated and re-engineered this regime to support LEO constellations. In May 2024 it issued a space radio business license to Guodian Gaoke for four satellites in the Tianqi LEO IoT constellation, and a wireless frequency license plus space radio station license to Tsinghua University for the Smart SkyNet-1 01 medium-orbit broadband test satellite. MIIT publicly characterized these as part of an effort to issue space station licenses and frequency permits in batches for low-orbit constellations rather than satellite-by-satellite.

On 8 November 2024 MIIT released for public comment the draft Interim Measures for Domestic Coordination and Management of Satellite Networks, with comments due 8 December 2024. The finalized Interim Measures were promulgated in early 2025 and took effect 1 May 2025. In August 2024 MIIT issued the Guiding Opinions on Optimizing Business Access to Promote the Development of the Satellite Communications Industry, a 19-point document with a 10 million-user satellite-connectivity target by 2030. The Guiding Opinions are the legal pretext under which MIIT subsequently granted satellite mobile services operating licenses to China Telecom, China Mobile, and China Unicom for direct-to-device service over Tiantong and successor systems.

subsections.heading: On the absence of a unified Space Law
paragraphs: As of end-2024 China still does not have a unified, promulgated Space Law or Regulations on Civil Space Activities. Drafting of an Atmospheric Space Activities Law has been on the State Council and NPC legislative agenda for years and was reactivated through 2024, partly in response to the Space Pioneer Tianlong-3 static-fire accident of June 2024, but no full State Council regulation under that title was issued in 2024. The operative 2024 regulatory output is therefore MIIT's draft Satellite Network Domestic Coordination Measures, MIIT's Satellite Communications Guiding Opinions, batched MIIT spectrum and station licensing for LEO constellations, the CNSA July 2024 Notice on Strengthening Quality Supervision of Commercial Space Projects, and the November 2024 Action Plan for High-Quality and Safe Development of Commercial Space, alongside the standing SASTIND Interim Measures.

sections.heading: Commercial launchers: 2025 to 2026 state of play
paragraphs: China's private launch sector has shifted from prototype hops to orbital reusability attempts in the last 18 months. Six companies define the field: iSpace, LandSpace, Galactic Energy, CAS Space, OrienSpace, and Deep Blue Aerospace. Each is now flying or assembling a follow-on vehicle aimed at the Guowang and Qianfan megaconstellation manifests. All operate under SASTIND Order No. 12 permits, layered over launch range allocation at Jiuquan, Taiyuan, Xichang, Wenchang, or the new Hainan commercial pad.

subsections.heading: LandSpace (Zhuque-2, Zhuque-3)
paragraphs: LandSpace remains the technical pacesetter. Its methalox Zhuque-2 became the first methane-fueled rocket to reach orbit in July 2023, and the upgraded Zhuque-2E (Y5) Block 2 flew on 14 May 2026. The headline event was the 3 December 2025 maiden flight of Zhuque-3, a stainless-steel two-stage methalox vehicle. The payload reached orbit, but an anomaly during landing-engine ignition caused the booster to crash near the pad. LandSpace will repeat the recovery test in Q2 2026 and target a reflight in Q4 2026.

subsections.heading: iSpace (Hyperbola-1, Hyperbola-3)
paragraphs: iSpace returned the solid-fueled Hyperbola-1 to orbit on 29 July 2025 from Jiuquan, placing the HS-9 satellite into a 500 km sun-synchronous orbit. The reusable methalox Hyperbola-3 is the strategic bet. The company opened a Wenchang processing and refurbishment hangar in October 2025, launched the Qinglan drone ship (42 m by 100 m, 40 m by 60 m landing area) in August 2025, and on 29 December 2025 released footage of preparations for a sea-based vertical takeoff vertical landing recovery. The maiden orbital flight is targeted for late 2026, with a Series C raise of roughly USD 99 million extending runway.

subsections.heading: Galactic Energy (Ceres-1, Pallas-1)
paragraphs: The solid Ceres-1 (Gushenxing-1) failed on 10 November 2025 when the fourth-stage orbital control engine shut down early. The series returned to flight on 15 January 2026 with a successful sea-launched Gushenxing-1S delivering four IoT satellites, before suffering a second loss two days later. The kerolox Pallas-1, with seven CQ-50 engines on the first stage and a 7-ton LEO capacity, was vertical at Jiuquan in May 2026 with the supporting pad complete. Galactic Energy disclosed a USD 336 million funding round earlier this year.

subsections.heading: CAS Space (Kinetica-1, Kinetica-2)
paragraphs: Owned by the Chinese Academy of Sciences, CAS Space has the cleanest record. The solid Kinetica-1 has flown repeatedly from Jiuquan since 2022; a sea-launched Kinetica-1H variant is planned for 2026. Kinetica-2, a 53 m, 625-ton, 766 ton-thrust medium-lift vehicle, successfully flew on 30 March 2026 from the Dongfeng Commercial Space Innovation Pilot Zone, delivering three satellites. Kinetica-2 is intended to carry the Qingzhou cargo craft to China's space station.

subsections.heading: OrienSpace and Deep Blue Aerospace
paragraphs: OrienSpace's solid Gravity-1, the world's largest solid-fuel orbital launcher, made a successful second flight from a sea barge near Haiyang on 11 October 2025. Four Gravity-1 missions are scheduled across 2026. The kerolox, partially reusable Gravity-2 (70 m, 4.2 m core, 5.2 m fairing) slipped to NET 2026 after first-stage engine hot-fire campaigns.

Deep Blue is the most VTVL-focused of the group. After the September 2024 10 km hop ended in a hard landing and a follow-up test in late July or early August 2025 reportedly ended in a crash, the company executed a nine-engine full-system static fire of the Nebula-1 first stage on 1 November 2025. Nebula-1A was placed on its transporter-erector at Haiyang's new liquid-propellant pad in March 2026.

sections.heading: Foreign investment restrictions: the 2024 Negative List and sectoral overlay
paragraphs: The governing instrument is the Special Administrative Measures for Foreign Investment Access (Negative List) (2024 Edition), promulgated jointly by the National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) and the Ministry of Commerce (MOFCOM) as Order No. 23, signed on 6 September 2024 after approval at the NDRC's 10th executive meeting on 8 April 2024. It took effect on 1 November 2024 and repealed the 2021 edition (Order No. 47). The 2024 edition cuts the national list from 31 entries to 29 and, decisively for industrial policy, eliminates every remaining manufacturing-sector restriction.

There is no entry in the 2024 Negative List that names aerospace, satellite manufacturing, launch vehicles, satellite TT&C, or remote sensing as restricted or prohibited categories. The manufacture of satellites, launch vehicles, propulsion components, ground equipment, and satellite TV reception equipment is, on the face of the list, open to wholly foreign-owned enterprises.

subsections.heading: List entries that touch the space value chain
bullets: Telecommunications: basic telecommunications services must be Chinese-controlled; value-added telecommunications services are capped at 50 percent foreign equity. This is the operative ceiling for satellite communications service operators, mobile satellite services, VSAT, and direct-to-device offerings.

Internet news, online publishing, online audiovisual, and cyberculture services are prohibited to foreign investors, which forecloses foreign-controlled satellite-delivered content and broadcast distribution.

Geodetic surveying, mapping, aerial photography for surveying, marine charting, and administrative-boundary mapping are prohibited to foreign investors. This sweeps in much of the commercial Earth-observation and remote-sensing data-services business when conducted over Chinese territory.

subsections.heading: The sectoral overlay that actually restricts foreign investors
paragraphs: Because the list is silent on space, the operative restraints come from the regulators that license each activity. SASTIND treats launch vehicles as weapons equipment for production-license purposes, and foreign equity in entities holding weapons and equipment scientific research and production licenses is in practice barred under the Regulations on Weapons and Equipment Scientific Research and Production Licensing. The Telecommunications Regulations of the PRC require Chinese state control of basic-telecom licensees, and MIIT's August 2025 Guiding Opinions on Optimizing Business Access, together with the CAC-led Management Regulations for Terminal Device Direct Satellite Service (effective 1 June 2025), assign licenses only to Chinese carriers. The Surveying and Mapping Law administered by the Ministry of Natural Resources, plus CAC data-export rules, gate cross-border transfer of remote-sensing imagery.

subsections.heading: The encouraged-list counterpart
paragraphs: Running alongside the Negative List is the Catalogue of Industries for Encouraged Foreign Investment, issued by NDRC and MOFCOM as Order No. 52, approved 26 October 2022 and effective 1 January 2023. The 2022 Catalogue lists aerospace-related items that qualify foreign-invested projects for tariff exemptions on imported equipment and tax incentives, including aero-engine and component manufacture, satellite-applications technologies, high-performance aerospace materials, and aviation auxiliary power systems. Encouragement is a fiscal benefit, not a license to operate.

sections.heading: What international operators need to know
paragraphs: International operators contemplating Chinese space business should read the regime as a stack rather than as a single regulator. The 2024 Negative List opens manufacturing on its face but the binding constraints sit off-list. Path-to-revenue analysis must enumerate every license a counterparty holds, every license a joint venture would need, and every regulator that signs off.

subsections.heading: Practical checklist
bullets: Identify the licensing pathway by activity: SASTIND Order No. 12 for launches, MIIT for spectrum, station, and services, MOFCOM and NDRC for foreign investment access, MFA and SASTIND for UN registration.

Build the application package nine months before launch. SASTIND's tightening notice requires the third-party liability insurance certificate, radio frequency license, debris mitigation plan, and payload identity data up front.

For satellite communications inside China, accept that basic-telecom services require Chinese control and that direct-to-device service is licensed only to China Telecom, China Mobile, and China Unicom.
For Earth observation over Chinese territory, treat the surveying and mapping prohibition as a hard line. Data export is additionally gated by CAC cross-border rules.

For manufacturing joint ventures, confirm whether the Chinese partner holds a weapons and equipment production license; foreign equity is barred in those entities even though the Negative List is silent.

Map the encouraged-list incentives. Aero-engine and component manufacture, satellite-applications technologies, and high-performance aerospace materials attract import-equipment tariff exemptions under Order No. 52.

Track the Atmospheric Space Activities Law (Hangtian Fa). Drafting was reactivated through 2024 after the June 2024 Tianlong-3 static-fire accident, and the final statute will likely consolidate licensing, registration, liability, and debris-mitigation rules currently spread across SASTIND, MIIT, and CNSA instruments.

subsections.heading: The drafting trajectory
paragraphs: Until the unified Space Law is promulgated, the operative texts are SASTIND Order No. 12 (2002) as supplemented by the early-2023 tightening notice, COSTIND and MFA Order No. 6 (2001) for registration, the MIIT Interim Measures for Domestic Coordination and Management of Satellite Networks (effective 1 May 2025), the MIIT Guiding Opinions of August 2024, the CNSA July 2024 Notice on Strengthening Quality Supervision of Commercial Space Projects, the November 2024 Action Plan for High-Quality and Safe Development of Commercial Space, NDRC and MOFCOM Order No. 23 (effective 1 November 2024), and NDRC and MOFCOM Order No. 52 (effective 1 January 2023). Together these instruments are the compliance perimeter.

faqs.question: Who is the lead space regulator in China?
faqs.answer: There is no single lead regulator. CNSA is the civilian public face responsible for policy, science missions, and international cooperation. SASTIND, which CNSA sits inside, holds the regulatory and licensing spine for civil launches, defense-industrial procurement, and the dual-use industrial base. MIIT controls spectrum, satellite radio station licensing, and telecom services market access. CMSA, reporting through the Central Military Commission's Equipment Development Department, runs human spaceflight. Foreign investment access is jointly controlled by NDRC and MOFCOM through the Negative List. A compliance analysis must address each gate by activity, not by single agency.

faqs.question: Does China have a national Space Law?
faqs.answer: Not yet. As of end-2024 China has no unified, promulgated Space Law or Regulations on Civil Space Activities. Drafting of an Atmospheric Space Activities Law (Hangtian Fa) has been on the State Council and NPC legislative agenda for years and was reactivated through 2024, partly in response to the Space Pioneer Tianlong-3 static-fire accident of June 2024. The operative regime is therefore SASTIND Order No. 12 of 2002 (as supplemented by the early-2023 tightening notice), COSTIND and MFA Order No. 6 of 2001 for registration, MIIT's Interim Measures for Satellite Network Domestic Coordination (effective 1 May 2025), and MIIT's August 2024 Guiding Opinions.

faqs.question: What is SASTIND Order No. 12 and what does it require?
faqs.answer: SASTIND Order No. 12, formally the Interim Measures on the Administration of Permits for Civil Space Launch Projects, was signed by COSTIND on 21 November 2002 and took effect 21 December 2002; SASTIND inherited administration in the 2008 reform. It requires every civil launch project to obtain a project-specific, non-transferable permit. Applicants must file nine months before the launch month, six months in advance where cross-departmental coordination is needed. SASTIND examines within 30 days and decides within 10 working days. Applicants must show compliance with Chinese law, treaty obligations, upstream radio licensing, technical and financial capacity, and mandatory third-party liability insurance.

faqs.question: What MIIT licenses does a commercial satellite operator need?
faqs.answer: A commercial satellite operator must obtain a layered set of MIIT authorizations administered through the Radio Management Bureau. The core instruments are a Radio Frequency Use Permit for the satellite's downlink and uplink bands, a Space Radio Station License for each on-orbit transmitter, a Satellite Earth Station Setup and Use License for gateways and user terminals (processed through the MIIT government-services portal), and where the operator sells connectivity, a telecom services market entry permit through the Telecom Business Market Integrated Management Information System. Materials demonstrating spectrum capability, completion of international and domestic frequency coordination, and conformity with the orbital filing are mandatory before launch.

faqs.question: What changed in MIIT's 2024 satellite communications rules?
faqs.answer: Through 2024 MIIT re-engineered the regime for LEO constellations. In May 2024 it issued batched space radio business licenses to Guodian Gaoke (Tianqi) and Tsinghua University (Smart SkyNet-1 01). On 8 November 2024 it released the draft Interim Measures for Domestic Coordination and Management of Satellite Networks for public comment due 8 December 2024; the final rule took effect 1 May 2025. In August 2024 MIIT issued the Guiding Opinions on Optimizing Business Access for the Satellite Communications Industry, a 19-point document with a 10 million-user connectivity target by 2030. These instruments support direct-to-device licensing for China Telecom, China Mobile, and China Unicom over Tiantong and successor systems.

faqs.question: Are foreign investors allowed in Chinese satellite manufacturing?
faqs.answer: On the face of the 2024 Negative List, yes. NDRC and MOFCOM Order No. 23, effective 1 November 2024, eliminates every remaining manufacturing-sector restriction. The manufacture of satellites, launch vehicles, propulsion components, ground equipment, and satellite TV reception equipment is open to wholly foreign-owned enterprises. However, the operative constraints sit off-list. SASTIND treats launch vehicles as weapons equipment for production-license purposes, and foreign equity in entities holding weapons and equipment scientific research and production licenses is in practice barred. Investors must therefore confirm whether a Chinese partner holds such a license before structuring a joint venture.

faqs.question: What space activities are prohibited to foreign investors in China?
faqs.answer: Three Negative List categories touch the space value chain. Basic telecommunications services must be Chinese-controlled, capping satellite communications service operators. Value-added telecommunications services are capped at 50 percent foreign equity. Internet news, online publishing, online audiovisual, and cyberculture services are prohibited, foreclosing foreign-controlled satellite-delivered content. Geodetic surveying, mapping, aerial photography for surveying, marine charting, and administrative-boundary mapping are prohibited, which sweeps in much of the commercial Earth-observation and remote-sensing data-services business conducted over Chinese territory. The Surveying and Mapping Law and CAC data-export rules reinforce the mapping prohibition.

faqs.question: Which Chinese private launch companies are operational?
faqs.answer: Six companies define the field through 2026: LandSpace (methalox Zhuque-2 and Zhuque-3, the latter attempting recovery in Q2 2026 after a 3 December 2025 landing-engine anomaly), iSpace (solid Hyperbola-1 returned to flight 29 July 2025, methalox Hyperbola-3 maiden flight targeted late 2026), Galactic Energy (solid Ceres-1 and kerolox Pallas-1 vertical at Jiuquan in May 2026), CAS Space (solid Kinetica-1 since 2022, medium-lift Kinetica-2 flew 30 March 2026), OrienSpace (solid Gravity-1 second flight 11 October 2025, kerolox Gravity-2 NET 2026), and Deep Blue Aerospace (Nebula-1 nine-engine static fire 1 November 2025, orbital recovery attempt targeted 2026).

faqs.question: What post-launch reporting does SASTIND require?
faqs.answer: Under the SASTIND tightening notice on civil space launch project licenses, license holders must file a project completion report within 30 days of launch, deorbit satellites at end-of-life, passivate rocket upper stages, give 30 days' notice for any life-extension request, and report orbital changes, fragmentations, or reentries. Uncovered third-party loss remains the holder's residual liability, and fraud or violation can trigger license revocation and criminal referral. Registration with SASTIND under COSTIND and MFA Order No. 6 of 2001 is the predicate for China's notification to the UN Secretary-General under the 1975 Registration Convention.

faqs.question: How does CMSA differ from CNSA?
faqs.answer: CMSA and CNSA were both established in 1993 but sit in different lineages. CNSA is administratively a bureau inside SASTIND, which is itself inside MIIT, and handles civilian policy, science missions, and international cooperation. CMSA is the specialized agency administering the China Manned Space Program (Project 921). It reports through the Central Military Commission's Equipment Development Department, not through SASTIND or MIIT. The astronaut corps is military, Jiuquan is a PLA range, and the Tiangong space station's command runs through Beijing Aerospace Control Center. CMSA coordinates with civilian players through a State Council and CMC joint leading group.

faqs.question: What is the Catalogue of Industries for Encouraged Foreign Investment?
faqs.answer: The Catalogue is issued by NDRC and MOFCOM as Order No. 52, approved 26 October 2022 and effective 1 January 2023. It runs alongside the Negative List and grants tariff exemptions on imported equipment and tax incentives to foreign-invested projects in listed categories. Aerospace-related encouraged items include aero-engine and component manufacture, satellite-applications technologies, high-performance aerospace materials, and aviation auxiliary power systems. Encouragement is a fiscal benefit, not a license to operate; the Negative List and sectoral regulators (SASTIND for launches, MIIT for spectrum, MNR for surveying) still gate market entry. Investors should read both instruments together.

faqs.question: Does China comply with the UN Registration Convention?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Measures for the Registration of Objects Launched into Outer Space were jointly issued by COSTIND and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs as Order No. 6 of 8 February 2001. Registration with SASTIND is the predicate for China's notification to the UN Secretary-General under the 1975 Registration Convention. The launch licensee is responsible for filing registration data after a successful launch. SASTIND Order No. 12 explicitly requires that the proposed launch not threaten China's obligations under the Outer Space Treaty, Registration Convention, or Liability Convention; treaty conformity is a substantive condition of permit grant.
